core_claim: RedSun (CVE-2026-41091) is a link-following flaw in Microsoft Defender's own file-remediation workflow. Defender runs as SYSTEM and can be redirected via an NTFS junction so its privileged write lands in a protected system directory. Released by Nightmare Eclipse as the second exploit in an April 2026 series; Microsoft patched it out-of-band on May 19–21, 2026 and CISA added it to KEV.

slides.subline: CVE-2026-41091. Link-following flaw in Microsoft Defender. CISA KEV. Patched May 21, 2026.

slides.on_slide_copy: Defenders trust Defender. Privileged writes from it rarely get a second look.
slides.subline: File operations from the Antimalware Service Executable (MsMpEng.exe) are treated as trusted. Detections targeting that process are rare in most endpoint baselines.

caption:
Nightmare Eclipse released RedSun as the second exploit in their April 2026 series. CVE-2026-41091. A local privilege escalation that turns Microsoft Defender's own remediation into an attacker-controlled write primitive.

The flaw is CWE-59: improper link resolution before file access. Defender's Antimalware Service Executable (MsMpEng.exe) runs as SYSTEM. During file remediation it performs privileged writes to paths it does not fully validate. An attacker with local access can place an NTFS junction so that write resolves into a protected system directory.

It is a race-condition class issue. Timing windows during Defender's cleanup workflow give the attacker the window to swap in the junction before the write completes.

Most endpoint tooling treats MsMpEng.exe file operations as inherently trustworthy. That blind spot is where this exploit lives.

Microsoft patched it out-of-band on May 19-21, 2026 in Malware Protection Engine v1.1.26040.8. CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 20, requiring US federal agencies to remediate by June 3. Huntress confirmed active exploitation in the wild.

Verify your Defender engine version. Check Windows Security, then Settings, then About. If it shows below 1.1.26040.8, trigger a manual update. For defenders: treat MsMpEng.exe file writes to protected directories as an alerting condition, not background noise.

Do you treat security tool file operations as trusted by default in your detections?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[RedSun, CVE-2026-41091, Nightmare Eclipse, Microsoft Defender, link following, CWE-59, Windows LPE, CISA KEV, MsMpEng, Defender remediation]
comment_prompt: Do you treat security tool file operations as trusted by default in your detections?
alt_text: Title card: RedSun turns Defender into the attacker. CVE-2026-41091, a link-following flaw in Microsoft Defender, patched May 21, 2026 and listed in CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. A Defender shield icon with a red glow bending outward toward a protected system directory.

RedSun is the second exploit Nightmare Eclipse released in their April 2026 series, targeting the Antimalware Service Executable. CVE-2026-41091 was assigned and Microsoft patched it out-of-band on May 19-21, 2026.

Defender's own file-remediation runs as SYSTEM and resolves NTFS junctions without verifying the destination. CWE-59, improper link resolution before file access, allows a standard user to reach a protected system directory through a redirected Defender write. One red file glows among white system files in the dark.

A race-condition class flaw: timing windows in Defender's cleanup workflow give an attacker the window to replace a directory with a junction before the SYSTEM-privileged write-back completes. A light arrow bends mid-air toward a protected core.

Most endpoint tooling treats MsMpEng.exe file operations as trusted background noise, not alertable events. That assumption is the gap RedSun exploits. A field of identical dark slabs with one pulsing red and otherwise indistinguishable.

Verify Defender engine version 1.1.26040.8 or later in Windows Security settings. Automatic updates push it silently but confirmation is needed on managed endpoints. Treat MsMpEng.exe writes to protected directories as an alerting condition, not background noise. A split scene: a patched Defender engine behind a green shield on one side, an unpatched version with a broken shield on the other.

Your security tool is not immune. When Defender's own file operations can be redirected, the protector becomes the write primitive. A Defender shield icon, one half intact and glowing green, the other fractured with a SYSTEM token escaping.
The call to action asks whether you treat security tool file operations as trusted by default in your detections. A single pulse of light on a clean dark end card.

sources.supports: CVE identifier; CWE-59 classification (improper link resolution before file access); CVSS 7.8 HIGH; affected engine versions up to 1.1.26030.3008; fixed in 1.1.26040.8; CISA KEV date added May 20, 2026; due date June 3, 2026.

narration.text: Nightmare Eclipse released RedSun in April 2026 as the second in a series of Microsoft Defender zero-days, and it does something counterintuitive: it turns Defender itself into the attacker's write primitive.

This became CVE-2026-41091, a link-following flaw in the Malware Protection Engine, patched out-of-band by Microsoft on May 21, 2026, and added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog the day before.

The category is CWE-59, improper link resolution before file access. Defender's remediation process runs as SYSTEM, and it resolves NTFS junctions without verifying whether the destination is still within a directory the attacker controls.

It is a race-condition class issue: timing windows during Defender's cleanup workflow give an attacker the opportunity to swap a directory for a junction before the SYSTEM-privileged write-back completes, landing the binary exactly where they need it.

Most endpoint tooling treats file operations from MsMpEng.exe as inherently trusted background noise, which means the write goes undetected in environments that have not explicitly built detections for privileged Defender process file activity.
The patch is an engine update, not a Windows update: verify that Defender engine version 1.1.26040.8 or later is deployed, especially on managed endpoints where automatic updates may be delayed.
Do you treat file operations from your security tool as trusted by default, or do you alert on them? Drop your answer in the comments.

CWE-59. Defender resolves junctions. Write hits protected dir.
Race-condition class. Junction swap. SYSTEM write lands.
MsMpEng.exe trusted. File ops invisible in most baselines.
Engine 1.1.26040.8. Verify. Alert on MsMpEng writes.
Security tool not immune. Protector becomes the primitive.
